input: Suppose that there are five patients in a hospital whose lives could be saved by the releasing of a certain gas, but that this will inevitably release lethal fumes into the room of another patient whom for some reason we are unable to move. Do you release the gas?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: Suppose that there are five patients in a hospital whose lives could be saved by the releasing of a certain gas, but that this will inevitably release lethal fumes into the room of another patient whom for some reason we are unable to move. Do you release the gas?

[Deontologist]: As a deontologist, I would not release the gas regardless of the potential consequences. The moral rule of non-maleficence (do no harm) takes precedence over any utilitarian calculation of saving more lives. We have a duty to protect the life and well-being of every individual in our care, even if it means sacrificing one person's life to prevent greater harm.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your commitment to non-maleficence, but I must respectfully disagree. In this scenario, releasing the gas would result in the death of two people, whereas keeping it contained would only lead to the death of one person. From a utilitarian perspective, it seems that releasing the gas would be the morally justifiable action, as it would minimize total harm and maximize overall well-being.
